summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Final hearing regarding a child of Aboriginal descent – Where the child’s mother is Aboriginal and does not currently have a relationship with the child – Where the child presently lives with the paternal grandmother and where this arrangement is to continue – Where the father currently spends limited time with the child supervised by the paternal grandmother – Where the paternal grandmother is highly critical of the mother and her family – Where the mother and the father pose some risks, of varying degrees and natures, in spending time with the child but which the court finds can be ameliorated – Orders made for the father to spend time with the child on a graduating basis and subject to the completion of named parenting courses – Orders made for the mother to re-establish a relationship with the child, first at a contact centre, and thereafter with the supervision of the mother’s support worker – Orders made for the paternal grandmother to make major long-term decisions for the child subject to a process of consultation – Where a relationship with the mother provides the child with the best opportunity to learn about and experience her Aboriginal culture and identity.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SIMPSON J:

These proceedings are in relation to the care arrangements for X, born in 2022 and now three years of age. X is Aboriginal. X currently lives with the paternal grandmother, Ms Roy (“the paternal grandmother”) who is not indigenous. X’s mother is Ms Manco (“the mother”). The mother is Aboriginal and is a member of the D people. X’s father is Mr Boels (“the father”). He is not indigenous.
At the time of the final hearing, the mother had not spent time with X since January 2024 and the father was entitled under orders, to spend a short period of time with X each week, at the home of the paternal grandfather where the father resides. The evidence makes clear that more recently, the father’s time has occurred at the home of the paternal grandmother.
The mother has a child of a previous relationship, E, born in 2018. The mother was also engaged in family law proceedings with E’s father, Mr F, in relation to the care arrangements for E. The final hearings for both matters were consolidated. The mother and Mr F reached agreement on the fifth day of the final hearing as to the parenting arrangements which best serve E’s interests. Those final orders were also supported by the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”). The same ICL represented both E’s and X’s interests at the final hearing.
Those orders provide for E to continue to live with his father and to spend time with the mother in the presence of her NDIS Case Worker, Ms B, both in Canberra, where E lives, and in City G, where the mother lives.
X and E have not lived together since late 2022. It was noted in the final orders with respect to E that the parties would engage with the paternal grandmother in the hope of securing opportunities for E to spend time with X in the future.
Upon the making of the final orders relating to E, Mr F took no further part in the proceedings.
I have regard to the final orders for E and the arrangements for time between the mother and E set out therein. The operation of those orders and the mother’s obligations to E create a practical limitation to the time the mother may otherwise have available to spend with X, in the event orders were made for such to occur.

BACKGROUND

The mother and father met sometime in 2020 and commenced a relationship shortly thereafter or in 2021. They separated in around December 2022/January 2023.
Following a series of events in late December 2022, X has remained in the care of the paternal grandmother after the mother provided X to the father. At the same time, the mother also asked Mr F to take care of E. In early January 2023, the father told the paternal grandmother that he could not manage caring for X and left the child in her care, where she has remained.
The mother has a number of vulnerabilities. The mother has been diagnosed with a mild intellectual disability (Exhibit 50) and is supported in her everyday living by NDIS Case Workers, attending with the mother for a number of hours, three days each week. At the time of the final hearing, Ms B had been providing this support on two of those days and had been working with the mother since 2023.
The mother commenced these proceedings on 27 March 2023, seeking a recovery order for X.
Interim orders were made on 28 April 2023 by consent, providing for X to live with the paternal grandmother and for X to spend supervised time with mother at the Contact Centre City G (“the contact centre”) for two hours each Saturday and with the father for two hours on a weekly basis (as agreed between the father and the paternal grandmother), with such time to be supervised by the paternal grandfather.
On 18 August 2023, further interim orders were made by consent which provided for the mother’s two hours of weekly time with X to become supervised by the mother’s NDIS Case Worker, Ms B.
Further orders were made on 26 October 2023 by consent, in circumstances where the implementation of the orders of 18 August 2023 did not proceed, which provided for the mother’s time with X to be supervised at the contact centre.
It is not controversial that the mother has spent very little time with X since she has been living with the paternal grandmother, notwithstanding the orders for supervised time. The mother had four occasions of time with X at the contact centre occurring on 11 August 2023, 2 December 2023, 16 December 2023 and 27 January 2024 (with the reports for those occasions becoming Exhibits 16, 17, 18, and 19, respectively). The supervision arrangements broke down after the short session on 27 January 2024, the circumstances relating to which are explained more fully later in these reasons.
While the interim orders for supervised time remained in place, X did not have any further time with her mother and as at the final hearing, X had not spent time with her mother for around 18 months.
This matter is characterised by deep mistrust between the parties. The mother regards the paternal grandmother as wrongfully retaining X, ignoring the mother’s requests for her return and placing little weight upon the benefits to the child of having a relationship with the mother. While the mother acknowledges the practical care of X provided by the paternal grandmother, she is suspicious as to her motivations and considers that X is being wrongfully kept from her, and her family and culture. The mother describes X’s removal from her as being akin to the experience of the “stolen generation”.
The mother is similarly critical of the father for his failure to secure the return of X to her and for his inability to counter the authority of his mother.
The paternal grandmother remains steadfast in her view that X is at risk in the care of each of the parents, though to differing degrees and for different reasons. The paternal grandmother asserts that the mother does not have the capacity to safely provide care to X and identifies a number of vulnerabilities which she says create an unacceptable risk for X in her mother’s care. Those vulnerabilities include asserted illicit drug use, unsafe alcohol consumption, unsafe employment choices, general parenting and functioning deficits (linked to the mother’s intellectual disability), housing uncertainty, emotional dysregulation and anger, and the risk of exposure to family and other violence. The paternal grandmother contends that those risks are unacceptable and cannot be mitigated, even by the intervention of supervision and other supports.
As is explained further below, the position of the paternal grandmother changed during the final hearing, where she moved to acknowledge and support the child in having time with her mother, before returning again to her complete opposition to the child spending any time or communicating with her mother.
The paternal grandmother supports the father having limited time with X only, supervised by her, unless otherwise agreed, and does not accept that he has the capacity to independently parent X. The relationship between the paternal grandmother and her son remains strained and there has been no real progression of the father’s time with X since orders were made on 28 April 2023. The parenting deficits of the father which the paternal grandmother identified in her discussions with the Family Consultant included his immaturity and his failure to comply with orders (including his failure to participate in the Child Impact Report interviews and about drug testing and completion of programs to assist in the development of parenting skills). The paternal grandmother also identified some risk that the father would facilitate X spending time with the mother outside the terms of orders made.
The father sought orders that would have him substantially involved in X’s care and assume responsibility for decision making for the child. While he proposed that such would occur in a defined time frame, by the conclusion of the hearing, the father modified his position, proposing a more modest final outcome in which he would provide regular care to X, unsupervised.
The mother had sought orders in her Initiating Application for X to live with her and spend limited day time periods with the father. That application, filed to commence these proceedings in March 2023, did not contemplate orders relating to the paternal grandmother despite the child living with her.
At the commencement of the final hearing, the mother did not produce a minute of final orders sought modifying her relief nor had she filed an Amended Initiating Application, contrary to directions made on 28 April 2025.
On the final day of the hearing, after the conclusion of the evidence, the mother’s counsel submitted that this was a matter where it may benefit the child if interim orders were made, instead of determining the matter on a final basis. A minute of interim orders sought by the mother was produced (Exhibit 47) and which I was advised had been circulated to the other parties prior to the commencement of the hearing that day. No minute of final orders was provided on behalf of the mother on that occasion, in the event the application for interim orders was not successful. It was accepted on behalf of the mother that she had no formal fall-back position. It was not submitted that the proposed interim orders ought to be made pending the delivery of the final reasons. Instead, on behalf of the mother, it was submitted that only interim orders should be made and that the matter would otherwise remain on foot and be adjourned for consideration and further case management at a later date.
The paternal grandmother, the father, and the ICL, opposed the making of interim orders at this stage of the proceedings. For the reasons which follow, I do not propose to make interim orders relating to the care of X.
The proceedings had been on foot for more than two years by the time of the final hearing. Aspects of the mother’s case were prepared very late, which I accept was, as submitted by her counsel, the consequence of very late Legal Aid funding being allocated. That does not however, explain why an application for the matter to proceed on an interim basis was not made prior to or at the time the matter was allocated final hearing dates. Directions for the preparation of the final hearing were made on 28 April 2025, four months before the commencement of the final hearing.
Where the mother was not spending time with the child despite extant orders providing for supervised time, no explanation was given on behalf of the mother as to why an application for interim relief (or the recommencement of supervised time under the existing orders) had not been made earlier, or even at the latest, on the first day of the final hearing.
I have regard to the overarching purpose of family law practice and procedure, as set out in s 95 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), and the need to conduct proceedings in a manner which promotes the best interests of the child. The timely resolution of these proceedings is essential, given X has been the subject of litigation for most of her life.
The Act also mandates that in child related proceedings, the court must have regard to the Principles as set out in Division 4 of the Act. Those principles include:
(a) The court is to have regard to the needs of the child and the impact of the proceedings upon the child (Principle 1);

(b) The court is to actively direct, control and manage the conduct of the proceedings (Principle 2); and

(c) The proceedings are to be conducted without undue delay and with as little formality and legal technicality and form as is possible (Principle 5).

I also have regard to my responsibility for the efficient use of the resources of the court and the impact an adjournment, and a subsequent additional final hearing, would have upon those resources, and indirectly, other users of the court (who may have their hearing delayed because of the continuation of this matter).
Each of the parties in these proceedings is in receipt of a grant of aid. Acceding to the mother’s application for interim orders to be made will result in further demands upon the finite and strained resources of Legal Aid. High Court authority makes clear that the court may have regard to the protection of public resources. In Aon Risk Services Australia Ltd v Australian National University (2009) 239 CLR 175, French CJ made clear at [23] that courts are required to be mindful of the wider public interest in ensuring that the limited court resources are applied and utilised effectively and efficiently.
I decline to make interim orders in this matter as I am not persuaded that doing so is in the best interests of X. It is highly undesirable that these parties should face the continuation of these proceedings, beyond the orders I make on a final basis. While I recognise the vulnerabilities of the mother and her difficult circumstances and her desire to rebuild connection with her daughter (which desire I accept is genuine), there are consequences for the choices made by parties in the conduct of their litigation. The application made on the mother’s behalf on the last day of the final hearing may have been more favourably received if made at a much earlier juncture in these proceedings. I am not prepared to impose upon this child (and those responsible for her care) further delay and uncertainty about her living arrangements.

THE POSITION OF EACH PARTY

The mother

In circumstances where I was not provided a final minute of orders sought by the mother and where the relief sought in her Initiating Application did not engage sufficiently with the reality of X’s longstanding care by the paternal grandmother, I will regard the mother’s interim orders sought as reflecting the final orders she seeks, but recognising that her ultimate relief was for the child to be returned to her care. At the conclusion of the evidence however, it was recognised by the mother’s counsel that such an outcome (for orders that X live with her mother) was not available on the evidence and that conclusion, no doubt in part informed the decision to instead seek interim relief only.
The mother proposed the resumption of supervised time between she and X, to occur at a professional supervision service before progressing to occur outside the centre, in the presence of the mother’s NDIS support person. She accepted, though on an interim basis, that X should live with the paternal grandmother and that the paternal grandmother shall have sole decision-making responsibility for X with respect to major long-term decisions, but subject to a process of consultation between the parents and the paternal grandmother. The mother’s interim minute of orders sought did not provide for time between the father and X. However, I do not take that to mean that she contends that the father should not spend time with X. The mother’s Initiating Application proposed time between X and the father and the mother’s evidence generally supported that occurring.

The father

The father’s final minute of orders sought was annexed to his case outline document (Exhibit 13) filed 26 August 2025 (the second day of the final hearing), with the minute becoming Exhibit 14. In that late amended document, he sought orders which would see him share decision-making responsibility with his mother and for X to live with her. He sought orders providing for a graduating regime of time between him and X, which, in six months’ time, would see X spend time with him for one overnight each fortnight and one evening in the alternate week but otherwise as agreed between him and the paternal grandmother.
The father supported X spending time with her mother but with such time subject to supervision, in a contact centre. After two years, as agreed between the father and the paternal grandmother, the father proposed that such time might then occur at the mother’s home or in the community, with such time to be supervised by the mother’s NDIS Case Worker or another applicable supervision service.

The paternal grandmother

The paternal grandmother’s case outline document (Exhibit 3) contained her final minute of orders sought. Those orders provided for the mother to have “no” (emphasis in original) time with X and for X to spend time with the father as agreed or otherwise fortnightly blocks of four hours, supervised at the home of the paternal grandmother. X would otherwise live with the paternal grandmother, and the paternal grandmother would be solely responsible for making major long-term decisions for X.
The position taken by the paternal grandmother with respect to the mother having no time with X was contrary to her proposed orders contained in her Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 12 February 2025. As is identified in more detail later in these reasons, the attitude of the paternal grandmother to the child’s relationship with her mother changed at various points in the hearing. When being cross examined, the paternal grandmother indicated support for the mother to resume supervised time with X and cross examination of the Family Consultant by the mother’s counsel, adopted that position.
Without adequate explanation, that position however changed again later that same day, when the paternal grandmother’s counsel indicated that his client resiled from her sworn oral evidence and reverted to opposing any time at all between the child and her mother, as set out in her written minute of orders sought.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer

The ICL’s initial position, which represented a “preliminary view ... only”, as contained in their case outline document (Exhibit 5), became Exhibit 6. That “preliminary view” provided for:
(a) The paternal grandmother to have sole decision-making responsibility in relation to major long term decisions;

(b) X to live with the paternal grandmother;

(c) The father to spend time with X as agreed and failing agreement, for three hours each week upon completion of named parenting courses; and

(d) The mother to spend time with X at a contact centre for up to two hours each month.

The ICL’s final minute of orders sought, as provided on the final day of the hearing, became Exhibit 53. On a final basis, the ICL proposed that X should live with the paternal grandmother and that the paternal grandmother should have sole decision-making responsibility in relation to all major long-term decisions for X, but subject to a process of limited consultation with X’s parents. The ICL sought orders providing for:
(a) The mother to spend time with X on a fortnightly basis, with such time to be supervised at a contact centre;

(b) For X to spend time with the father as agreed between the paternal grandmother and the father, and failing agreement, in a graduating regime (until he was caring for X for a weekend each fortnight and for an afternoon each week); and

(c) The father’s care of X to be subject to the completion of named parenting courses by the father.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The mother relied upon:
(a) Her Initiating Application filed 27 March 2023;

(b) Her affidavit filed 11 August 2025; and

(c) Leave was granted to the mother to rely upon an affidavit of her NDIS Case Worker, Ms B, which was only completed and served on the second day of the hearing. I accepted the submissions of the mother’s counsel with respect to the challenges experienced by the mother’s solicitor in securing a grant of aid and the late allocation of that grant. The mother’s reliance upon the affidavit of Ms B was not opposed.

The father relied upon:
(a) His Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 5 August 2025; and

(b) His affidavit filed on the morning of the first day of the final hearing, 25 August 2025.

The paternal grandmother relied upon:
(a) Her Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 12 February 2025;

(b) Her affidavit filed 4 July 2025; and

(c) Her affidavit in reply to the mother’s affidavit, filed 20 August 2025.

Ms H (“the Child Impact Report writer”) had prepared a Child Impact Report, dated 11 July 2023, and which became Exhibit 10. Ms J (“the Family Consultant”) thereafter prepared a Family Report, dated 27 May 2024, and which became Exhibit 8.
The mother, the father, and the paternal grandmother were each cross examined, along with the Family Consultant and Ms B.
I have read and observed the evidence of each of the parties and witnesses, and I have carefully considered each of the documents tendered throughout the course of the final hearing and which became exhibits.
In the Family Report, the Family Consultant identified the benefit of securing further information about the mother’s engagement with third parties to aid in the assessment of risk more generally and made certain preliminary recommendations (Exhibit 8, at [69]-[77]). Despite the Family Report being more than 12 months old at the time of the final hearing, those further inquiries had not been undertaken. Efforts to address some of those evidentiary gaps were belatedly made on behalf of the mother prior to the commencement of the hearing (including as noted with respect to the evidence of the NDIS Case Worker).
There are other limitations in the Family Report with respect to engagement with issues relating to the child’s aboriginality and cultural identity, which are addressed further below.
I am conscious that each of the parties was the recipient of a grant of aid. I infer that funding limitations and, or, late funding allocations may explain some of the gaps in the evidence in this matter, particularly with respect to s 60CC(3) and s 61F of the Act. However it has occurred, the unfortunate reality remains that relevant evidence to assist the Court to make orders in the best interests of this child has not been obtained. I am not inclined to impose an adjournment of the proceedings on the parties, with a direction that additional expert evidence is obtained, in circumstances where I identify other risks to the child from continuing exposure to litigation. I also recognise that it simply may not be possible for the identified evidence to be obtained given the scarcity of resources in legally aided matters to meet anticipated expert evidence costs and the reality that there may be few experts available to undertake work of this nature.
I have resolved on this basis that I must do the best that I can with the evidence available to me at this time and I will not impose upon the parties and the child, further uncertainty by the extension of these proceedings.

ISSUES TO BE DETERMINED

The child’s right to enjoy her Aboriginal culture

The mother is an Aboriginal woman, of the D nation. The father and the paternal grandmother are not indigenous.
Recognition of the importance of the child’s Aboriginal culture and her right to connect with her culture, family, community, country and language is enshrined in the Act. It is a separate and distinct best interests’ consideration to be assessed in every matter involving an Aboriginal child (s 60CC(3)). Section 60CC(3) was inserted into the Act as part of the amendments introduced under the Family Law Amendment Act 2023 (Cth).
The Explanatory Memorandum (Family Law Amendment Bill 2023) makes clear that the particular needs and cultural rights of Aboriginal children are to be acknowledged and focussed on when considering their best interests and that the concepts of family and kinship are to be incorporated in determining care arrangements (at [6]). The Explanatory Memorandum recognises that connection to culture is a significant protective factor for the wellbeing of Aboriginal children and their families (at [49]) and that the court is required to consider the child’s right to enjoy their Aboriginal culture by having the opportunity to connect with, and maintain, their connection with their family, community, culture, country and language, and the court is to reflect upon the likely impact any proposed parenting order will have on that right (at [48]).
While the amendments to the Act have refined focus upon these important rights for Aboriginal children, similar considerations have been codified in the Act for some time. Prior to the amendments, such considerations were set out as follows:
60CC How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests
...

(3) Additional considerations are:
...
(h) if the child is an Aboriginal child or a Torres Strait Islander child:
(i) the child’s right to enjoy his or her Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture (including the right to enjoy that culture with other people who share that culture); and

(ii) the likely impact any proposed parenting order under this Part will have on that right;

...
Right to enjoy Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture

(6) For the purposes of paragraph (3)(h), an Aboriginal child’s or a Torres Strait Islander child’s right to enjoy his or her Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture includes the right:
(a) to maintain a connection with that culture; and

(b) to have the support, opportunity and encouragement necessary:

(i) to explore the full extent of that culture, consistent with the child’s age and developmental level and the child’s views; and

(ii) to develop a positive appreciation of that culture.

This matter was referred to the Indigenous List of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) in orders made on 28 April 2023.
In orders made on 18 August 2023 (which predate the amendments to the Act, referred to above) relating, in part, to the preparation of a Family Report, the Family Consultant was asked to assess, among other things:
The impact of any order on the child’s right to enjoy his or her Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture (including the right to enjoy that culture with other people who share that culture).
The Family Report is dated 27 May 2024 and the Family Consultant’s engagement with the parties occurred in March and May 2024. The Family Consultant obtained from the parties their “views” with respect to the child’s Aboriginal culture and while it is noted that each of the paternal grandmother and the father expressed their “support” of the child’s culture, how that support was in a practical sense to be advanced was not made clear.
The Family Report does not address the specific term of reference in the orders of 18 August 2023 with respect to the impact of particular care arrangements on the child’s right to enjoy her Aboriginal culture. While it is clear that the Family Consultant gave priority to consideration of certain identified risks for X and the prioritisation of her safety, other risks for X including loss of access to her culture and Aboriginal identity were not adequately explored. I do not know if the Family Consultant considered these inquiries to be outside her expertise, but she does not make a declaration to that effect. Regrettably, the Family Consultant does not respond to or address that part of the terms of reference.
Section 60CC(3) of the Act, as it now stands, is expressed in mandatory terms:
Additional considerations—right to enjoy Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture
(3) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) the child’s right to enjoy the child’s Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture, by having the support, opportunity and encouragement necessary:
(i) to connect with, and maintain their connection with, members of their family and with their community, culture, country and language; and

(ii) to explore the full extent of that culture, consistent with the child’s age and developmental level and the child’s views; and

(iii) to develop a positive appreciation of that culture; and

(b) the likely impact any proposed parenting order under this Part will have on that right.

The mother identifies in her outline of case document as a “significant issue”, the separation of the child from the mother and the possible impact of such upon the child’s current and future connection to culture, family and custom. The mother was identified as the child’s only connection to her D culture.
The outline of case document of the paternal grandmother, settled by counsel who appeared on her behalf at the final hearing, fails to engage at all with those considerations, notwithstanding prompting about s 60CC(3) being embedded in the approved form. That was a troubling omission which was not adequately explained.
While the father’s outline of case document identified the importance of X’s cultural identity, the significance of such was identified as necessarily yielding to consideration of the child’s safety. The risk, and potential consequences for the child, of loss of her connection to her culture and identity were not articulated. There was no particularisation from the father in his evidence as to how he would in fact support X’s cultural identify “through ongoing cultural engagement” as summarised in that document.
The ICL identified that one of the matters to be determined was the extent to which the paternal grandmother and the father could support X’s Aboriginal heritage.
While the parties were in dispute about long term decision making and parental responsibility for X, I have no evidence nor submissions which would assist in the determination required under s 61F of the Act:
In:
(a) applying this Part to the circumstances of an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child; or

(b) identifying a person or persons who have exercised, or who may exercise, parental responsibility for such a child;

the court must have regard to any kinship obligations, and child‑rearing practices, of the child’s Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander culture.
I respectfully refer to and commend the comprehensive summary of the evolution of s 61F of the Act as set out by Schonell J in Hronn & Torleif (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 821. While the nature and extent of the evidence presented in that matter differs markedly from the circumstances in this case, the analysis undertaken by His Honour there provides a very useful framework to guide parties and practitioners in matters where the rights of an Aboriginal child to experience her culture are being considered.
The paternal grandmother is responsible for the care of two other children, being the children of one of her daughters, with whom she has had no contact for some years. Those children are also Aboriginal (through the paternal line) and have lived with the paternal grandmother since 2018. The paternal grandmother deposes to her care of those children being the product of care proceedings.
As reported to the Family Consultant, the paternal grandmother asserted that she has been “approved” as a carer of Indigenous children (presumably by the Department of Communities and Justice). There is nothing in the written evidence about the details of that process of approval nor about any cultural awareness and sensitivity training undertaken by the paternal grandmother.
I have no evidentiary basis upon which to assess the cultural sensitivity and competence of the paternal grandmother nor the way that the paternal grandmother proposes to foster X’s connection with her culture. The paternal grandmother opposes X spending any time with her mother and does not propose orders for X to have any relationship at all with any member of the maternal family. That position alone sits at odds with the assertion that she values and will support the child’s cultural identity.
In her comments to the Family Consultant, the paternal grandmother was highly critical of the extended maternal family. The basis upon which the paternal grandmother had formed these adverse opinions about the maternal family was not made clear.
The conduct of the paternal grandmother’s case, including the disregard for the statutorily mandated considerations relating to Aboriginal children weighs against an assumption of cultural competence.
The mother spoke of her cultural heritage with the Family Consultant (Exhibit 8 at [22]):
[Ms Manco] stated that she identifies as an indigenous woman of the [D people]. She stated that she connects with her totem of [...]. [Ms Manco] stated that her Aboriginal heritage and identify were important to her. She stated that she would like to teach her children dreamtime stories and take them to cultural events on offer. [Ms Manco] stated that she is in contact with aunties and local elders who can assist her in this undertaking. [Ms Manco] stated that in the past she made Aboriginal necklaces and painted, wanting to return to this in the future. [Ms Manco] stressed that both [X] and [E] were of Aboriginal heritage, wanting them to grow up connected, aware and proud of their culture. [Ms Manco] expressed in emotional terms that it was her belief that [X] was ‘part of the stolen generation’ being removed from her care, in particular blaming the paternal grandmother for this taking place.
The mother identified in her oral evidence that she wanted X to be connected to “her other [...]”, being the D people.
The mother reports the importance to her of the care she had received as a young person from her aunties (particularly when her mother was not able to provide safe and appropriate care) and the grief she experienced, upon the death of the aunties, including in the lead up to the events of December 2022, when X came to be placed in the care of the paternal grandmother.
The mother’s engagement in mourning those family members she says, impacted upon her ability to provide care for X. It is possible that the mother’s response to and the impact of the death of these important carers upon the mother, should have been the subject of separate, culturally sensitive assessment and consideration.

Other issues to be determined

The ICL set out in her outline of case document a summary of the issues to be determined in this matter. At the conclusion of the evidence, it was agreed that X would remain living with the paternal grandmother. Drawing upon the summary undertaken by the ICL and having regard to the evidence and the requirements of s 60CA of the Act, the most pressing issues remaining to be resolved, in addition to what arrangements sufficiently address X’s rights under s 60CC(3), may be summarised as follows:
(a) How should long term decision-making for X be held between her parents and the paternal grandmother (and implicitly, should an order be made allocating parental responsibility for X to the paternal grandmother);

(b) Having regard to the concessions made on the mother’s behalf by way of her minute of orders sought, in what way, if any, is identified risk for X in the mother’s care made acceptable, such that X may have a relationship with the mother; and

(c) What risks, if any, arise from X spending unsupervised time with the father and if risk is identified, whether such risk is unacceptable and if unacceptable, how such can be ameliorated to an acceptable level.

In determining what orders are in X’s best interests, in addition to the considerations in s 60CC(3) (as set out above), I must also have regard to the considerations at s 60CC(2) and s 60CC(2A), as follows:
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and
(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

CONSIDERATION

The final hearing continued across seven days. The hearing was conducted largely over audio visual link, save for some counsel (and Mr F) being present in Canberra for the hearing (the final two days were heard in Sydney).
The mother has a number of vulnerabilities, including a mild intellectual disability, and as at July 2024, she was described in an NDIS Progress Report as having some difficulties regulating emotions and interpretation of what others communicate to her – requiring support to express her needs in a way that is constructive and less likely to lead to conflict (Exhibit 50). I granted the mother leave to have a support worker present with her while she gave her evidence.
The mother was subject to cross examination across two days and briefly on another separate day to permit cross examination relating to late evidence. There were occasions where, either prompted by objection from her counsel or based on my own concerns, I required questions of the mother to be re-framed in order to ensure they were fair and more likely to be understood by the mother. I am satisfied however, that the mother was generally able to understand what was being asked of her and was able to answer questions once they had been broken down or explained further to her. On one occasion the mother became frustrated and terminated the connection. The mother rejoined the link moments later and apologised for her actions. On another occasion, the mother identified that she needed to take a break and that opportunity was given to her.
The mother answered questions briefly and in simple terms. I am satisfied that the mother was a truthful witness and she did her best to recall events which had occurred sometime in the past and which in some instances, were difficult for her to revisit.
The father also gave his evidence remotely. There were occasions during his oral evidence when the father expressed frustration with the experience of cross examination and, or, with respect to the proceedings more generally. On one occasion, the father also abruptly terminated his audio-visual connection as he expressed his unwillingness to continue with the process. The matter was stood down to enable the father’s advisors to speak with the father. The hearing resumed a little while later and the father subsequently apologised for his conduct.
I recognise that the experience of cross examination can be uncomfortable. However, the father’s conduct was suggestive of some immaturity upon being asked to be accountable for his actions and when being challenged about his sworn written evidence.
During cross examination, the father abandoned parts of his evidence, including a number of assertions made in his affidavit which were critical of the mother and were suggestive of the risk she might pose if having unsupervised care of children. The father’s abandonment in cross examination of parts of his own written evidence also impacts the evidence of the paternal grandmother. The paternal grandmother makes serious allegations against the mother, in some instances based primarily on her account of things she asserted she had been told by the father. As a consequence, I find that the evidence of each of the father and the paternal grandmother was unsatisfactory in part. I set out in further detail below, my other concerns with respect to aspects of the paternal grandmother’s evidence, which was generally highly critical of the mother.

Safety considerations

The legislation requires the court to make orders that are in the best interests of X, and which promote her safety and the safety of those people responsible for her care. This requires the court to look to the future to identify the arrangements which are determined to be in her best interests. Consideration of, and fact findings about, historical allegations are necessary only to the extent to which such findings inform the assessment of prospective risks of harm for the child (Pickford & Pickford [2024] FedCFamC1A 249 (“Pickford”) at [79]-[87]).
Both the mother and the father propose that the mother resume time with X, subject to supervision. The mother makes this concession, not because she accepts the assertions of the paternal grandmother that the child would be at risk in her care but acknowledging the reality that she does not presently have a relationship with X (and related challenges for X if unsupervised time was to occur).
The paternal grandmother opposes any time between the mother and child occurring.
In these circumstances, some analysis of the evidence relating to the underlying risk that the paternal grandmother identifies, is necessary, in particular to assess the extent to which such risk may be ameliorated and given the concerns I hold generally as to the nature and reliability of the evidence upon which the paternal grandmother relies in support of her contentions as to risk.
The paternal grandmother and the father each assert that the child is at risk in the mother’s care, of physical and emotional neglect (including as a consequence of the mother’s asserted drug and alcohol use), exposure to family violence and emotional or psychological harm. The paternal grandmother says that those risks are unacceptable and that they cannot be mitigated whereas the father supports the mother having supervised time with the child.
There is much conflict in the parties’ differing accounts of the relationship between the parents and the circumstances of their separation and events leading to X coming into the care of the paternal grandmother. In circumstances where X has lived with the paternal grandmother since December 2022, the resolution of the historical dispute between the parties about how that came to pass is not strictly required.
The paternal grandmother obtained an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order (“ADVO”) protecting her from the conduct of the mother, which was made final in May 2023, for two years. That order has expired. That application was made following events at the home of the paternal grandmother in January 2023 when the mother attended there. It is clear on the account of the paternal grandmother and the mother, that the mother sought the return of X and the mother became upset. The police attended.
The father contends that he attempted to calm the mother. The father does not allege that he was assaulted by the mother on that occasion, but the paternal grandmother makes that allegation. The mother was not charged with assault of the father and the video which the paternal grandmother asserted she held of the events was not in evidence.
The father was convicted of assaulting the mother following events which occurred between them prior to their separation but which were referred to the police by the mother in April 2023. The matter was heard at a Local Court in September 2023, and the father was found guilty of the offence. Consequently, an ADVO was issued to protect the mother from the father in September 2023.
The father was also charged with driving under the influence of drugs in August 2023. The father was fined and his licence was disqualified for three months (commencing from January 2024).
A series of statements are made in the paternal grandmother’s written evidence which are highly critical of the mother, and upon which the paternal grandmother relies to suggest ongoing use of violence and difficulty in self-regulation by the mother. It was contended by the paternal grandmother that the mother had been the subject of “an alarming amount” of police attention because of her conduct, creating potential risk for the child (including, inferentially, even if supervision of that time occurred).
The bases of these statements about the extent of police engagement with the mother are unclear. Two examples illustrate the deficiency of this evidence.
The paternal grandmother asserted (based, she says, on reports made to her by her son) that the mother and her child, E, had been involved in a serious incident with a man, Mr K, with whom it was contended the mother was then in a relationship. It was asserted in some detail that the child had thrown water on Mr K, that he retaliated, that subsequently boiling water was thrown on Mr K by the mother and that Mr K required urgent medical attention.
The paternal grandmother then asserts (at [87] of her affidavit filed 4 July 2025):
... This alarmed me significantly. As [E] [sic] was present throughout this incident. [E] [sic] was exposed again to domestic violence in the mother’s care at presumably, first opportunity of unsupervised time, since residing with his father, by witnessing this incident, at high level risk of unintentional injury. Adding further evidence for the need for continued supervision from a trusted source such as a formalised supervision service to prevent unsupervised contact with [E] [sic] being repeated and exposed to further domestic violence from the mother and unsafe environments.
The father in his oral evidence denied that he knew anything about these events and denied that he had provided information to his mother about those alleged events (as she claims).
The review of a document relating to an ADVO taken by the police to protect the mother reveals a very different series of events. Those documents make clear that the mother was assaulted by Mr K, that she then sought the assistance of police and that orders were made for her protection from Mr K. The mother was clear in her evidence, which I accept, that E was not in her care when Mr K assaulted her.
The documents make clear, and I find, that the mother was the victim of violence in those events and that she took appropriate steps to obtain protection in the face of that violence. The mother declared that she has had nothing further to do with Mr K. There was nothing about the manner in which the mother answered questions about this event or Mr K which lead me to question the veracity of her answers. I accept her evidence.
It remains troubling that the paternal grandmother was prepared to make very serious allegations in her sworn evidence, against the mother, with scant regard for the veracity of the matters asserted and despite having access to other documents which suggested a different set of events. The paternal grandmother exhibited to her affidavit a copy of an ADVO dated May 2025 made in favour of the mother, against Mr K (at [88]).
The paternal grandmother also asserted in her written evidence that the mother had “an alarming amount of criminal, anti-social violent associated charges throughout 2023-2024”.
That part of the paternal grandmother’s written evidence listed a “summary of offences” relating to the mother. Review of the records makes clear the extent of the “engagement” of the mother with police or court processes related to the ADVO proceedings between the mother, the paternal grandmother and the father only. The interpretation of various “court lists” by the paternal grandmother (and, or, her advisers) was misguided. I am satisfied that the paternal grandmother shaped that evidence to convey a criticism of the mother which was not properly founded.
In his oral evidence, the father abandoned and, or, rejected parts of his own written evidence, including:
(a) He said that he had not seen the mother stealing property in the presence of E;

(b) He agreed that he had not read the reports from the contact service and his evidence about what had occurred during supervised visits between the mother and child were based on what his mother told him (including his statement that the mother had been “banned” from attending the contact service);

(c) He denied any knowledge of the alleged Mr K incident and denied having a discussion with his mother about it;

(d) He denied, contrary to his written evidence, that he had seen the mother and members of her family together abusing illicit substances;

(e) He abandoned his written evidence that the mother had prioritised her drug addiction over essential needs for the children;

(f) He, in part, blamed his lawyers for the way his affidavit was written;

(g) He denied that the mother had consumed alcohol during her pregnancy with the child and said he saw her take marijuana “a few times”, contrary to his written evidence that she had used “large quantities” of marijuana. He denied knowledge of other drug use;

(h) He denied that the mother had assaulted him on a daily basis while they lived with his mother, saying this had happened once only;

(i) He denied that the mother had assaulted him at his father’s home in the presence of the child;

(j) He denied any recollection of the violence which, in his affidavit, he said caused him to take E to the paternal grandmother’s home;

(k) He denied his account in his affidavit that the mother had screamed at him because she did not have money for drugs or alcohol on 30 December 2022;

(l) He resiled from his evidence that the mother had never taken the child to see a doctor;

(m) While the father makes critical statements in his written evidence about the maternal grandmother (that she “lives in a dangerous environment”), he admitted that he had not been to her home during the currency of the proceedings; and

(n) He rejected any concern that the mother would not provide food for the child.

As a consequence of the deficiencies identified above, with respect to that part of the evidence of the paternal grandmother and the father, which is critical of the mother, I place little weight on any complaints they make which are not verified by independent records or by independent third parties.
The paternal grandmother asserts that the child is at risk of experiencing the mother becoming upset and, or, dysregulated and that such risk is unacceptable and cannot be mitigated. The mother and those working with her (including her NDIS Case Worker, Ms B) identify that the mother has experienced challenges in regulating her emotions, particularly anger. The mother deposes to having taken steps to learn more about how she experiences strong emotions and how she might respond differently in those events.
The mother attends upon a behavioural therapist each four months (as part of her ongoing NDIS plan) and she was able to articulate things she learned to do when feeling angry or upset about things. This included taking time to breathe and to step away from the matters which were impacting her. The engagement in and completion of support programs by the mother is acknowledged and the mother has evidenced a commitment to trying to learn how to respond differently when faced by challenges. This evidence was corroborated by Ms B who has observed the mother implement some of these learnings.
I accept that evidence. I am satisfied that the mother remains interested in learning about, and is working on improving, her self-regulation.
As to her mental health more generally, the mother admitted attending upon a doctor in April 2023 when she described low mood and was diagnosed with depression (which followed upon the mother no longer having either of her children in her care, in the circumstances set out previously). The mother’s evidence was that she found the anti-depressants trialled by her to be unsatisfactory and she in due course stopped taking them. The mother maintained that she was feeling better at this time and that the other supports she was engaged in, including learning about meditation, were assisting her and she did not require anti-depressant medication at this time.
The events on the last occasion that the mother had time with the child at the contact centre were the subject of some attention in the proceedings and the paternal grandmother expressed concern about the risk of such events being repeated (again in support of her contention that supervision did not sufficiently protect the child from risk in the mother’s care). It is clear on the evidence that the experience for X that day was unsatisfactory and that she became distressed by the mother’s abrupt and premature attempts to engage her attention and affection. The mother did not respond well to the encouragement and direction of the supervisors and the session ended early, at the mother’s direction.
The paternal grandmother describes her observations of X after these events and the child’s continuing distress and state of unsettlement which followed. I accept that evidence which was unchallenged. The paternal grandmother sent emails to the contact service after these events, expressing her observations of X and her concerns about what had occurred.
The paternal grandmother also shared her views about these events with the Family Consultant, and the Family Consultant spoke with a person or persons from the contact service. The Family Report at [56] includes
A discussion occurred between the Report Writer and staff from the [C Contact Centre]. Information was provided that the service has been suspended due to aggressive, inappropriate and emotive responses from the mother with concerns raised in relation to the impact upon the child and staff safety. Staff indicated some reservation in resuming supervised maternal visits until the mother had completed recommended counselling and could demonstrate an ability to act in a calmer and child focused manner.
The mother spoke to the Family Consultant about these events (as at [25]). She acknowledged that the arrangements were suspended in January 2024 but “denied acting in an aggressive or inappropriate manner” and “I thought I was calm, I don’t remember, I was not angry, [X] got upset, the worker comforted her not me, I ended up walking out”.
Review of the report made by staff at the contact centre (Exhibit 19) does suggest that the mother’s engagement there was perhaps insensitive to the experiences of the child on that occasion. It was noted in those records that the mother was eager to engage with the child and was perhaps too demonstrative too quickly (noting it had been some weeks since the last visit). When the child began to cry, the supervisor notes that the mother says “nup, this is sad. This is just sad” and “she doesn’t even know who I am. It’s because I am black”. Appropriate reassurance was given to the mother by the staff.
The report of that occasion makes clear that the mother found the session difficult including because the child was not responsive to her overtures. The mother eventually declared that she wanted to leave, after only 20 minutes. Nothing in the report suggests however that the mother’s behaviour was “aggressive” or abusive of staff.
The contact centre staff did not immediately suspend the mother’s engagement with the service, as has been contended by the paternal grandmother. It appears from the documents produced from the service that the communication to the contact service by the paternal grandmother of her subsequent observations of the child may have in part informed their decision at that time to suspend their involvement.
The records from the contact service are more in line with the mother’s account of what happened that day. It is difficult to reconcile the summary in the Family Report that “the service has been suspended due to aggressive, inappropriate and emotive response from the mother with concerns raised in relation to the impact upon the child and staff safety” (as at [56]) with the mother’s account and the records of the service. I am not satisfied that the mother’s conduct at the service could be described as “aggressive, inappropriate and emotive” and I do not accept there is a basis to contend that the mother acted in a way which might imperil staff safety.
I also have regard to the reports for the three earlier occasions of contact between the mother and the child which indicate the beginning of positive engagement between the child and her mother.
Those reports (Exhibits 16, 17 and 18) suggest on those occasions that the mother was child focussed, attentive to, and receptive to, the direction and encouragement of the staff and that positive engagement between the mother and the child occurred. Those observations support the contention that time between the mother and the child could be facilitated in a way which was developmentally appropriate and safe for the child and from which the child would derive opportunity to develop a relationship with the mother.
The records of the contact service, taken as a whole, and the mother’s evidence of her experiences there give me confidence that the resumption of supervised time at the contact service remains a feasible option. I understand because of more recent inquiries by the ICL that the contact service remains open to assisting this family again but given the time which has passed since their last engagement, the usual intake process will need to be repeated.
The paternal grandmother asserted that the child had been neglected while in the mother’s care, including in the days when the parents and child lived with the paternal grandmother and then in December 2022 when the father brought the child to the paternal grandmother’s home. The mother denies the descriptions of her care of the child.
The father also asserted that the mother failed to adequately care for X (and E) and that he was left concerned and worried for the welfare of the children in her care. I place little weight on the father’s evidence about these matters given the unreliability of his evidence as identified previously. The resolution of these disputed historical facts is not required given that each party supports X living with the paternal grandmother. However, it is appropriate that I record in these reasons that the independent evidence (Exhibit 34), of a doctor who assessed X (with the mother) in November 2022, was that she was “doing well” and no concerns were identified. Similarly, I have regard to the notes of staff from L Family Services who attended the mother’s home in August and October 2022 (Exhibits 23 and 24 respectively) and observed the child in the mother’s care. No adverse observations are expressed on those occasions.
I also observe that while the paternal grandmother and father contend that X was in a very grave state of health in December 2022, she was not sufficiently ill that either adult took her for emergency medical assessment at that time. The paternal grandmother secured a telephone appointment with a general practitioner, and a course of antibiotics was prescribed. No follow up appointment occurred, and the child was not directly examined by a doctor proximate to her being placed by the father in his mother’s care. The absence of any such medical review weighs against acceptance of the evidence of the paternal grandmother and the father as to X’s state of health at that time
The paternal grandmother was also reluctant to ascribe any responsibility to her son for the asserted neglect of the child in the lead up to these events, despite the reality that he was living with the mother at the relevant time, and he claimed to be the parent providing the primary care of X. The paternal grandmother contended in her oral evidence that she made allowance for her son given he was at that time in employment.
In these circumstances, the evidence does not allow a finding that there was a deficiency in X’s care prior to December 2022.
The paternal grandmother points to the mother’s periods of homelessness as indicative of further risk for the child. The mother and the father while still in their relationship together had periods where they were unhoused. The paternal grandmother asserted that her son was welcome to return to live with her on those occasions, or that he could also live with his father. The paternal grandmother did not accept that this circumstance was the same as the mother relying upon accommodation with family members.
It was suggested to the paternal grandmother by counsel for the mother that she applied a double standard when it came to her expectations of the parents – expecting a great deal of the mother while ignoring her vulnerabilities and expecting little of her son while ignoring his comparative advantage. While that contention was rejected by the paternal grandmother, I accept that there is inconsistency in the response of the paternal grandmother to the challenges each of the parents have experienced.
In any event, the mother is now settled in secure accommodation and lives on her own. She is also employed. Ms B attends the mother’s home on two occasions each week. She describes the home as clean and tidy and that the mother capably attends to the upkeep of her home.
Counsel for the paternal grandmother was unable to identify how the risks identified by the paternal grandmother relating to the mother’s care of the child would not be mitigated by the imposition of supervision of her time.

Any views expressed by the child

X is an infant. It was not contended that her views would be relevant.

The needs of the child

The Family Consultant in the Family Report outlines as follows:
[X] is a young toddler who has experiences challenges in her young life including compromised parenting, spending substantial time in the care of her paternal grandmother following her birth, placement with the paternal grandmother in [December 2022], restricted and disrupted time with her mother over the past seventeen months and limited, supervised time in paternal care. [X] was described by the paternal grandmother as a child who has been traumatised by her experiences of maternal care. [Ms Roy] further described [X] as strongly attached to her, seeking constant reassurance and requiring a high level of child focused care. [Ms Roy] stated that [X] is reliant upon her to provide stability in her living arrangements and emotional security. The particular vulnerabilities of [X] appear to be acknowledged by the father. [Ms Manco] appears to have limited understanding of [X’s] needs and the impact of compromised parenting. Note is made that there are child protection reports indicating that both [X] and [E] have been placed at risk whilst in past maternal care. [X] appears to be a bright, emotionally responsive and healthy child, achieving expected developmental milestones for her age. [X] was directly observed to be watchful, cautious and seeking the comfort of her paternal grandmother when in unfamiliar and stressful environments.
The emotional needs of [X] require careful consideration and priority in this matter. There are substantial issues of concern in respect to maternal parenting and personal insights. It is considered that it would be of assistance to the court for the mother to provide reports from her treating drug and alcohol service provider and anger management and mental health counsellor, this providing additional information about her engagement and demonstrated changes in her comprehension and understanding. It is also considered that it would be of benefit to the court that an assessment report be provided, this outlining the maternal diagnosis in relation to her intellectual disability and impact upon her functioning, behaviours and personal capacities and vulnerabilities. A report from [C Contact Centre] would assist the court in outlining the serious concerns observed by staff at this service and reasons for suspension of maternal arrangements.
...
On balance, it is considered that [X] will benefit from stability in her living arrangements, this being a continuation of care provided by the paternal grandmother at this time. It is acknowledged that [X] will have developed a primary attachment to her paternal grandmother and will require sensitivity and understanding with a gradual and planned changes in paternal arrangements indicated. The paternal grandmother is encouraged to facilitate opportunities for her son to parent and care for [X] in a more independent manner. It will be important for [Ms Roy] to provide information to the father in relation to [X], this also assisting [Mr Boels] to develop confidence, competence and insight into his young daughter’s individual needs.
The paternal grandmother has very competently provided for all of the child’s physical and day to day needs and I accept the evidence of the Family Consultant that a clear attachment bond exists between the paternal grandmother and the child. There is no challenge at this time to the continuation of the paternal grandmother’s care of X.
The relationship between X and her father is still developing and there are clear benefits to the child of that relationship being nurtured such that those benefits may be realised. The Family Consultant had suggested in May 2024 that formal supervision of the father’s time with X might be relaxed (at [67]) to “assist in [X] developing a closer attachment with her father, provide the opportunity for the father to engage in a parenting relationship and interactions with [X], whilst ensuring that this child feels comfortable and secure”.
The father’s time with the child has not progressed since the release of the Family Report. The oral evidence of the paternal grandmother and the father makes clear that their relationship has not improved and the paternal grandmother was not satisfied with the father’s time happening at the home of her ex-husband. In her oral evidence, the paternal grandmother said that she had to leave that location because of the father’s “outbursts” and she determined she was “no longer prepared to expose myself or [X] to that”. The paternal grandmother has then imposed upon the father her requirement that any time he has with the child will occur at her home and under her supervision.
The father revealed in his oral evidence that his ability to spend time with the child is controlled by the mother. The father said that it can be difficult for him to get to the mother’s home during the week given his work commitments and that she does not permit him to attend later than 4.00 pm because of her expectations around the evening routine for the children. The father said that he had not seen the child “for a while” given these challenges.
Neither the paternal grandmother or the father provided written evidence about the changes to the arrangements for the father’s care of X and that deficiency was unexplained.
It appears that the paternal grandmother remains untrusting of her son and she identified in her oral evidence her concern that he would facilitate X spending additional time with her mother.
Given the status of the relationship between the father and his mother, the continuation of an arrangement which requires the father’s time to occur at the paternal grandmother’s home, under her supervision, is likely to stymie opportunities for X’s relationship with her father to develop. I accept it is in X’s best interests for her relationship with her father to progress and for him to assume a greater role in providing for her care.
The Family Report does not identify the benefits to the child of having the opportunity to experience safe and consistent time with the mother. I am able to infer however, that there are many benefits for the child in having the opportunity to experience a relationship with her mother (see U & U [2002] HCA 36; (2002) 211 CLR 238 at [176]).
The child has a right to experience her heritage as a member of the D people and to learn about and connect to her culture and country. The orders sought by the paternal grandmother do not address those rights.
I am satisfied that the only real prospect of the child experiencing her culture and Aboriginal identity will be through her mother.

The capacity to provide for the child’s needs

I am satisfied that the paternal grandmother is very capably providing for X’s daily care and that the child experiences being part of a loving and connected household, with the paternal grandmother, her cousins and the extended paternal family.
Those arrangements are not subject to change and will serve X well at this stage of her life.
Where I remain concerned however, is the extent to which the paternal grandmother has capacity to support X’s relationship with her parents.
In final submissions, counsel for the paternal grandmother failed to address in any meaningful way the state of the evidence (which did not support the contentions being made on behalf of his client as to risk). There was no engagement with the complex challenges this child might experience in the event she was to be denied any opportunity for a relationship with her mother and culture. Counsel for the paternal grandmother conceded that supervision of the mother’s time would mitigate the risks which the paternal grandmother identified in the mother spending time with the child. He was however, still instructed to press an outcome which would deny the child any time with her mother.
The evidence and formal position of the paternal grandmother leaves me entirely unsatisfied about her capacity to meaningfully support the child’s relationship with the mother. I am satisfied and find that the paternal grandmother places little value upon the benefits which the child might derive in connection with her mother, even if that time was supervised. At best, the paternal grandmother evinces a limited understanding of the impacts upon the child of being denied the opportunity of any relationship with her mother, extended maternal family and with her culture and Aboriginal identity.
I also hold reservations as to the capacity of the paternal grandmother to support the father’s relationship with the child.
The paternal grandmother has exercised a degree of control over his relationship with the child which appears to have limited opportunities for the relationship to progress. The relationship between the adults, on each of their accounts, is strained. The fact that the father’s time with the child is now only occurring in his mother’s home and in her presence speaks of the paternal grandmother’s control.
It is also clear that the father failed to respond appropriately to the recommendations made by the Family Consultant about things he could do to foster and advance his position (for example, by the completion of the identified courses).
In circumstances where allegations were made about drug use by both parents, orders were made, on at least two occasions, for the father to engage in random drug testing. The father failed to do so. In his oral evidence, when asked why he had ignored court orders for drug testing, the father said that he felt his “character” was being attacked. That was an indulgent response and was suggestive again of immaturity and indicates indifference to the obligations created by court order.
The father admitted to some cannabis use and asserted that he had been prescribed medicinal marijuana, to address poor sleep. Despite a call for the script, no document was produced. While declaiming problematic drug use, the father was charged with driving under the influence of an illicit drug in August 2023 and consequently lost his licence in January 2024.
The father maintains that in his current employment, he can be subject to random drug testing.
The father’s evidence about his drug use and his attitude to court orders for testing was unsatisfactory.
The father also displayed frustration and immaturity about his engagement in these proceedings. Despite his long-term application that he should be responsible for X’s primary care, the father failed to participate in the Child Impact Report interviews. He later asserted that he was interstate but did not take steps to make himself available by any means, even telephone. Special and additional arrangements were also made for the father to be observed with the child for the purposes of the Family Report when the father did not make himself available as originally directed.
At the time of the final hearing, the father had completed a Triple P Parenting course. He explained in his oral evidence that he had been referred to a mental health service but discontinued attending after a couple of sessions as he found the engagement “unhelpful” when discussions had turned towards “weird things” including experiences in his family of origin.
That immaturity was also evident when the father terminated his video link during cross examination, noted previously. While the father later rejoined and apologised for his conduct, in response to questions from me about whether those events were suggestive of limited impulse control, the father said that he “always” controlled himself around his child and he had learned about and applied aspects of stoicism. That evidence is somewhat challenged by the evidence of the paternal grandmother that she experienced “outbursts” from the father which caused her to require his time to occur at her own home. Those matters were not explored further in cross examination, but I am left with some concern about the ability of the father to regulate himself.
I am not satisfied that the father has acquired greater insight into the responsibilities of parenting during the course of these proceedings and that he continues to experience impatience relating to his desire to assume a greater role in X’s care. While I accept that his relationship with X is important to her (as identified by the Family Consultant), I am also satisfied that the father needs to take additional opportunities to develop his parenting skills to be better prepared to provide care of X.
The mother has taken steps to develop her parenting knowledge and skills (completing parenting courses including Self-Care in Tough Times, What’s Behind the Behaviour, and Triple P Parenting Program). Ms B said that she observed the mother’s improvement towards being able to communicate more complex emotional needs.
I accept the mother’s evidence that she is responsibly engaging with her treaters and providers, and she derives benefit from the continuing assistance of her NDIS Case Workers.
The mother admits to continuing cannabis use (with a prescription for such) and describes her reliance upon it as being in part related to her stress and mood management.
The mother was involved in an incident in 2021 through which she suffered a serious injury to her leg and was hospitalised. The mother admitted to taking Panadeine Forte, only when she experienced additional pain in her leg. She said that she only took the drug sometimes and hadn’t taken it “for a while”. This evidence is consistent with the drug test results of the mother from July 2025 (opiate use was not revealed in other tests), and I accept her account of her use of that medication.
The mother undertook drug testing on three occasions, the most recent of which occurred in July 2025 (Exhibit 32) and indicated cannabis use and opiate use. The mother said that she had been unable to undertake other drug tests, despite requests of her to do so, due to the costs of them. I accept that evidence.
Ms B indicated in her oral evidence that she had not seen the mother use cannabis while she is with her, nor had she observed “illicit substances” in her home. Ms B had not observed the mother consume alcohol.
I am not satisfied that the mother’s admitted cannabis use or use of Panadeine Forte presents a risk to the child which is not sufficiently mitigated by supervision.
The mother has now secured independent accommodation. I accept her evidence (corroborated by Ms B) that she lives independently and manages her own care, with the assistance of the NDIS supports. Ms B made clear that the mother attends to her own self-care, maintenance of her home (which she described as very neat) and undertakes her own shopping and financial management. The NDIS team assist her in transportation to her various commitments.
Ms B in her oral evidence admitted that she had observed the mother to swear but that was “always” in the context of her “frustrations of matters with the children”. When the mother was upset, Ms B has seen her apply anger management responses (calming herself). Ms B also recounted occasions when the mother has been upset regarding the proceedings and arrangements for the children, including crying when supervised time with X concluded.
Ms B was an impressive witness. The extended period in which she has worked with the mother means that the mother is trusting of Ms B and she is an important support for the mother.

The benefit to the child of having a relationship with each of her parents and other people who are significant to her

The child’s very important relationship with the paternal grandmother will continue on each party’s application. Through that relationship, the child will remain connected to her cousins, with whom she lives, and other members of the paternal family.
There is benefit to X having a relationship with her brother, who now lives with his father in Canberra.
I am satisfied that there is a real benefit to this child in again being afforded the opportunity to safely develop a relationship with her mother and for greater opportunity to be given for her to strengthen her relationship with her father.
At the moment, the child has no relationship with her mother nor any of the extended maternal family. The paternal grandmother offers no solution to this deficiency.
Insufficient regard has been given to the risks created for this child’s wellbeing and emotional development if she was to experience the continuing severance of her relationship with her mother, the maternal family, and with her Aboriginal heritage.

Right to enjoy Aboriginal culture

I place significant weight on the reality that the child’s best chance of developing connection to, and experience of, her Aboriginal heritage will be achieved by supporting the child’s relationship with the mother. The mother’s counsel submitted, and I accept, that the child’s opportunity to connect to her culture will be derived significantly from connection with her mother, her mother’s family and her direct connection in that way with country, kinship and totems and that such cannot be facilitated by the paternal grandmother.
The orders sought by the father and by the mother each identify those benefits, by maintaining the child’s relationship with her mother.

DETERMINATION

Decision-making and parental responsibility
