X will remain living with the paternal grandmother. The parents hold parental responsibility for X. I propose to make orders that the paternal grandmother will also hold parental responsibility for the child, given X will live with her. I was not addressed about orders which would remove parental responsibility from the parents. In the circumstances of this case, where the three adults will remain involved in the care of the child, I will not make such an order.
I am satisfied that long term decision-making for X needs to involve a consultative process by the adults interested and involved in X’s care but that the final decision will be made by the paternal grandmother. It is especially important that the mother be able to have appropriate input to matters which are central to the child’s Aboriginal identity and culture.
I will make orders which require the paternal grandmother to provide information about a proposed long-term decision to each of the parents and for them to respond to her, with their views and, or, suggestions, within a defined time frame. The paternal grandmother may then make the decision and shall advise the parents of the decision, once made.
In this process, X will benefit from each of her parents playing a role in long term decision-making. The framework of consultation creates certainty such that the paternal grandmother will ultimately make the decision after receiving and considering the contributions from X’s parents.

The mother’s time with X

In considering what orders are in the best interests of the child, I am required to undertake an assessment of those arrangements which promote the safety of the child and of those who provide care of her, including from being subjected to or exposed to family violence, abuse, neglect or other harm. I am also required under s 60CG of the Act to ensure that any order I make does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.
Counsel for the paternal grandmother conceded that the provision of supervision adequately addressed many of the risk assertions identified by his client.
I take into account the contentions made about the parties’ experience of family violence. The paternal grandmother has had the protection of family violence orders against the mother, and those orders have expired at this time. Similarly, the mother has had the protection of an ADVO against the father. I also take into account that the father was convicted of assaulting the mother.
I have found aspects of the evidence of the father and paternal grandmother to be unsatisfactory particularly with respect to allegations made against the mother. The relationship between the adults remains poor. The orders I propose to make will limit opportunities for physical contact between the mother and the paternal grandmother or the father. I am satisfied that there is little risk of the repeat of the behaviours which occurred in early 2023 given the passage of time, that the mother has undertaken courses relating to behaviour management, and because the reintroduction of the mother’s time with the child will occur within the framework of supervision.
The orders sought by the mother for the recommencement of supervised time with the child are sensible and adequately respond to the risks identified by the paternal grandmother about the mother’s vulnerabilities and shield the child from the potential consequences of such. The implementation of spend time orders will require a degree of co-operation and support from each of the adults interested in the care of this child. I am satisfied that the parties engaging again with the contact service to provide their professional services will provide an appropriate framework for the careful reintroduction of the child to her mother and meet any remaining risk considerations addressed previously in these reasons.
The parties had previously endorsed Ms B as a suitable supervisor of the mother’s time with the child (though I acknowledge that consent was given prior to the last occasion of contact in January 2024). Ms B has confirmed in her oral evidence that she remains ready to assist the mother and would be prepared to attend periods of time between the mother and the child. Ms B provides equivalent support to the mother when she has time with E.
Ms B endorsed in her oral evidence, which I accept, that she would intervene in the event she observed the mother to respond to the child in a manner which was not appropriate, or if she saw indicators of the mother becoming dysregulated.
Another advantage of having Ms B present with the mother when she has time with the child is that Ms B is likely to be able to reassure the mother about the experience and to assist her to accept the likely slow process of building connection with the child. Ms B’s presence is likely in that way to be of assistance also to the child.
However, I accept the Family Consultant’s evidence that the specialist training and expertise of the staff at the contact service will be directed to the needs of and vulnerabilities of their child clients. That expertise will be invaluable given the complex challenges of the process of supporting X to become reacquainted with her mother and especially in the early phase of that process.
For these reasons, I propose to grant liberty to the mother to invite Ms B to attend the contact service with her, including during any preliminary intake process and for the purposes of her supervised time with the child. I will also make an order permitting the parties to provide a copy of the orders and these reasons to Ms B (and, or, subsequent NDIS Case Workers) and the contact service to ensure that they are appraised of the issues which have informed the decisions I make.
For the reasons given above, it is appropriate and protective of the child that the mother’s time with the child initially occur at the contact service. While there is no evidence to guide the measure of time necessary to best establish the child’s confidence in spending regular time with the mother, some caution is warranted given how long it has been since the child has seen her mother and the unsatisfactory circumstances of their last contact. However, the limitations of spending time at a contact service are well understood (for children and adults) and there is an appreciable financial burden which attaches to the service.
I have determined that the mother’s time with X under the supervision of the contact service should continue for not less than 10 occasions, which may mean a period of 20 consecutive weeks, from the date of commencement. This enables an extended period in which the mother’s time with the child is regularised and is occurring in the company of her Case Worker but with the additional child focussed expertise of the contact service staff. It is also possible that the contact service may not be able to facilitate contact with the regularity as is presently anticipated. If this occurs, it may take a little longer for the first 10 occasions of supervised contact to occur.
After the mother and the child have experienced 10 occasions of time at the contact service, the mother’s time with X may thereafter occur in the company of, and under the supervision of, Ms B, or such other person as may be agreed between the mother and the paternal grandmother in writing. This degree of flexibility takes into account the possibility that Ms B may not remain engaged in assisting the mother. If no alternate supervisor can be obtained, then the fall back shall be to return to the contact service (to ensure that the child is not at risk of experiencing another disruption to her time with her mother).
I am satisfied that these arrangements of having Ms B involved to assist the mother sufficiently address the risks previously identified, including the mother’s limited recent experience in caring for the child. The fact that the mother has formed a trusting relationship with Ms B may be an important factor in reassuring the mother and enabling the success of these processes for the child.
Once the mother’s time has progressed beyond that being supervised by the contact service, greater opportunity exists for X to enjoy time with her mother in locations which create opportunity for different experiences. This will enable the mother to plan and provide experiences which are child focussed and developmentally appropriate and which respond to the age and interests of the child. This flexibility also means that the mother may use this time to ensure that X experiences aspects of her culture, guided by the mother and elders and other people important to the mother.
Similarly, once X and her mother are regularly spending time together, I am persuaded that it is beneficial to X that her mother is also able to attend events at kindergarten or school to which parents are invited. In due course, there may be activities arranged by the child’s school which focus upon Aboriginal peoples and their culture. It is especially important for X that her mother can be there to participate or observe, as applicable.
The father may also attend those kindergarten and, or, school events and that may occur immediately (given his relationship with X is more established).
In the difficult circumstances of this case and given the limits in the evidence as identified previously, I am not persuaded that it is possible at this time to project further about what care arrangements between the child and her mother will be in the best interests of the child in the longer term. The child is still very young and her relationship with her mother is not presently established but the orders I make will enable a foundation for the establishment of that relationship.
In doing so, I also recognise that by her relationship with the mother, the child is given the best opportunity to learn about and experience her Aboriginal culture and identity. While I give weight to this consideration, I am satisfied that in doing so, orders have been made which sufficiently address the risks identified in these reasons.
While the mother and child remain engaged in time occurring at the contact centre, a fee will be incurred. Given the importance to the child of having the opportunity to develop and experience a relationship with her mother and in doing so, to more directly experience her culture, I have determined that all three parties should contribute to the costs of the contact service. The mother has limited financial means, derived from her employment (for which she said she receives $240 per week) and from Commonwealth benefits (for which she said she receives approximately $600 per fortnight). The father’s income was less clear, but he stated that he was (at the time of the hearing) employed. I don’t know what child support the father pays to the paternal grandmother to assist in meeting the costs of X. I accept that the mother does not pay child support to the paternal grandmother.
While the paternal grandmother carries the financial burden of the care of the child, she is a woman of some means, owning her home outright and also owning investment properties (subject to mortgages). The paternal grandmother had also indicated an intention to return to her employment in the later part of 2025, once X commences pre-school.
I require the parties to equally contribute to the costs of the supervision service. This will also guard against supervised time not occurring in the event that one party alone could not meet the costs levied. It would not be in the best interests of this child if she was denied an opportunity to develop a relationship with her mother because the mother could not afford to pay for the costs of the contact centre.

The father’s time with X

The ICL sought orders that the progression of the father’s time should be conditional upon his completion of nominated parenting courses. The father’s counsel in effect supported the making of that order in circumstances where it was conceded the father had not undertaken those programs, despite such being recommended.
The father has previously refused to comply with orders. In this instance, the ICL proposes that the progression of the father’s care of X be conditional upon his completion of specific courses and that making such a prerequisite for the progression of time may provide sufficient motivation for compliance. I will make those orders, given there is undoubted benefit to X in her father experiencing additional learning opportunities with respect to the challenges of parenting and child development. I accept the father’s evidence that he has completed a Triple P Parenting course. The Family Consultant specifically recommended that the father complete a Circle of Security Course and I will include the completion of that course as a pre-requisite for the progression of the father’s care of X, in addition to the two other programs as sought by the ICL.
The father modified his final orders, at the conclusion of the evidence, appropriately responding to the oral evidence of the Family Consultant and her concerns about X’s preparedness to rapidly increase her time away from the paternal grandmother.
The orders I make shall allow for the immediate extension of time between the father and X on Saturday each weekend, with such to occur with the substantial attendance of his father or mother. The paternal grandfather was not a witness in the proceedings. No submissions were made about that fact. Where the paternal grandfather has previously provided support and supervision of the father’s time with the child, but where his attitude about this into the future is not known to the court, the paternal grandmother may alternatively be the person to provide that support to the father.
In her oral evidence, the Family Consultant urged some caution in the progression of the father’s care of X, suggesting that the child needed an opportunity to become accustomed to longer periods in her father’s care before contemplating overnight time. I am conscious that at the same time, X will be experiencing the re-introduction of time with her mother. Proceeding cautiously in these circumstances is warranted and in the child’s best interests.
After three months, the requirement for the paternal grandfather or grandmother to be in substantial attendance will be removed (and subject to the completion of named parenting courses) and the father’s care of X will extend and will also embrace time on an afternoon in the alternate week. After 12 months from the making of the orders, X’s time will increase to include overnight time each alternate weekend. While this progression is slower than that sought by the father, it accords more closely to the recommendations of the Family Consultant. After a further six months, the father’s time with X on the Sunday, after her overnight stay, will also be extended to embrace the better part of that day.
I have included orders as to Mother’s Day and Father’s Day to ensure that X experiences time with her parents on those days. There may be other special occasion time for X with her parents, but specific orders were not sought by either of the parents and those matters will be subject to future management by the parties.

Communication with X

Each of the father and the mother will have the opportunity to communicate with X on one occasion each week. In the mother’s instance, this should await the conclusion of the time occurring face to face with the benefit of the contact service. The success of this order for X will depend upon the mother ensuring that she makes the necessary contact on the stipulated day and time and for the paternal grandmother to facilitate and support X in receiving the communication. Similar provision will apply to the father but given the status of his relationship with X, that communication may begin immediately.
I have made orders which will require the parties to exchange information necessary to enable this and for the paternal grandmother to do those things necessary to ensure X is available and supported in having this contact. I have expressed the orders with reference to a commencement time, recognising that while the child is young, it is especially not sensible to attempt to set time limits around the duration of the communication. Each of the adults involved in her care will need to remain mindful about the challenges for a young child in remaining attentive in these circumstances.

Restraints

I have made certain restraining orders, adopting the mother’s version, in circumstances where both parents and the ICL proposed that some orders to guide the conduct of the parties is warranted. The adults involved in X’s care will be expected to engage with each other respectfully. There are very clear benefits to the child of experiencing the important people in her life acting in this way.
In light of the father’s unsatisfactory evidence about his drug use, I will impose a protective measure such that the father be restrained from using illicit substances during, and 48 hours prior to, any period in which he is to provide care to X.

Communication between the parties

The use of a parenting app for the purposes of communication between the parties was sought by the ICL. This means of communication, namely AppClose, reflects the platform which has been previously nominated. The mother had expressed a preference for communication via telephone call but until the relationships between the adults improve, the use of a parenting app remains sensible, save for situations of emergency.
I have made additional orders which require the parties to communicate with each other in the event of an emergency relating to the child. Similarly, to ensure that the mother and the father obtain information relevant to the child’s progress, development and about her attendances upon health practitioners, further orders are made directing the paternal grandmother to share information with the child’s parents. The benefits to the child of ensuring the adults responsible for her care are aware of matters relating to her wellbeing are self-evident and I include orders authorising the parties to obtain information from the health practitioners and schools.

Other

The changeover arrangements for the child to spend time with her parents will largely rely upon the paternal grandmother to transport the child, a reflection of the living arrangements for the child. Once the mother is spending time with the child not linked to the contact service, the parties may agree a different changeover arrangement. Failing agreement, I have nominated the location of the contact service to minimise the risk of uncertainty and dispute.
The default arrangement for changeover between the paternal grandmother and her son is the continuation of what is currently occurring.
I have made orders which permit the paternal grandmother to obtain a passport for the child and to travel internationally with her. No submissions were made about these matters but orders relating to international travel were sought by the paternal grandmother and the ICL. However, while the child’s time with her parents is developing and progressing, international travel with the paternal grandmother shall not be prioritised over the child’s time with the parents, unless otherwise agreed with the parents.
The ICL sought an order that the paternal grandmother provide to the mother photographs of X on a monthly basis. I make such an order but to also include an update in writing as to X’s progress. This may assist in ensuring that the mother feels greater connection with the child, in addition to the spend time and communication orders I make. That information may assist the mother in her discussions with the child.
A range of further orders were sought by each of the parties in their respective cases. Many of those orders sought were not the subject of submission and I decline such orders in those circumstances and, or, where I am of the view that they are not necessary to support X’s best interests and, or, they have been sufficiently covered by the other orders I make.
